content.full_text:
Office of the Secretary of State
March Fong Eu

Executive Office
1230 J Street
Sacramento, California 95814

(916) 445-6371

January 10, 1990

Ms. Jeanne Pritchard
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804

Re: California Voter Foundation

Dear Jeanne:

I am requesting informal advice regarding any reporting or
other responsibilities arising under the Political Reform
Act resulting from the involvement of the Secretary of
State in the California Voter Foundation.

The Foundation was organized last year as a non-profit,
tax exempt entity to receive non-public money to
supplement voter registration outreach and educational
efforts historically conducted by the Office of the
Secretary of State. (See enclosed copy of articles of
incorporation.) Such efforts include bus signs, bill
boards, bill stuffers, public service announcements, a
toll-free registration form request number, placards,
contests, private-sector registration drives (McDonald's,
Granny Goose, utility companies, etc.) Such drives are
purely non-partisan. Traditionally, private contributions
have been made directly to the State but the Foundation
concept was thought to be a simpler and more efficient
mechanism. (This approach is similar to that used by such
entities as Friends of the Archives, Friends of the Parks,
etc. to enlist private-sector money to further public
projects.)

The Secretary of State was listed as the "incorporator"
with the then Chief of Elections, the then Media Director
and me as the initial officers. The Secretary of State
has had no direct personal involvement, however, in either
creating or organizing the Foundation. The only money

received thus far has come from participants in a seminar
on voting last year. No substantial money has been spent
and expenditures will be deferred pending receipt of your
informal advice.

Given the organization and purposes of the Foundation, do
any reporting or other responsibilities arise under the
Political Reform Act given the status of the Secretary of
State as an elected official and candidate for office? If
so, would any such responsibilities be eliminated by
removing the Secretary of State, formally and otherwise,
from involvement in the administration of the Foundation?
We wish to further the mandate of the Foundation without
compromising in any way obligations arising under the
Political Reform Act.

Your early, informal advice in these matters would be
greatly appreciated.

Sincerely,

ANTHONY L. MILLER
Chief Deputy

Enclosure

1699185

State
of
California
OFFICE OF THE SECRETARY OF STATE

CORPORATION DIVISION

I, MARCH FONG EU, Secretary of State of the
State of California, hereby certify:

That the annexed transcript has been compared with
the corporate record on file in this office, of which it
purports to be a copy, and that same is full, true and
correct.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, I execute
this certificate and affix the Great
Seal of the State of California this

MAY - 2 1989

[Great Seal of California]

March Fong Eu

Secretary of State

SEC/STATE FORM CE-107

1639183

ARTICLES OF INCORPORATION

ENDORSED
FILED
In the office of the Secretary of State
of the State of California

OF

CALIFORNIA VOTER FOUNDATION

MAY 01 1989

MARCH FONG EU, Secretary of State

I

The name of this corporation is the CALIFORNIA VOTER FOUNDATION.

II

A. This corporation is a nonprofit public benefit corporation and is
not organized for the private gain of any person. It is organized
under the Nonprofit Public Benefit Corporation Law for charitable
purposes.

B. The specific purpose of this corporation is to provide supplemental
support for voter registration and education programs of the
California Secretary of State.

a) The Corporation may solicit and receive gifts, legacies, devises
and conveyances of real and personal property for the purposes
of initiating and furthering voter registration and education
programs.

b) To that end, the Corporation may receive, hold and handle real
and personal property, sell, lease or otherwise dispose of same
including disposal through gifts to the State of California
either as directed by the donor or conveyor of such property or
in such a manner as the Board of Directors may determine
commensurate with the purposes of the California Secretary of
State's voter registration and education programs.

III

The name and address in the State of California of this corporation's
initial agent for service of process is:

Anthony L. Miller, Chief Deputy Secretary of State, 1230 J St.
Sacramento, California 95814.

IV

A. This corporation is organized and operated exclusively for chari-
table purposes within the meaning of Section 501(c)(3) of the
Internal Revenue Code.

B.  No substantial part of the activities of this corporation shall
consist of carrying on propaganda, or otherwise attempting to
influence legislation, and the corporation shall not participate
or intervene in any political campaign (including the publishing
or distribution of statements) on behalf of any candidate for
public office.

V

The property of this corporation is irrevocably dedicated to charitable
purposes and no part of the net income or assets of this corporation
shall ever inure to the benefit of any director, officer or member
thereof or to the benefit of any private person.  Upon the dissolution
or winding up of the corporation, its assets remaining after payment,
of all debts and liabilities of this corporation shall be distributed to a nonprofit fund, foundation or corporation which is organized and operated exclusively for charitable
purposes and which has established its tax exempt status under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code.


DATED:  April 5, 1989


                    [illegible]
            (Signature of Incorporator)


                    MARCH FONG EU
            (Typed Name of Incorporator)


I hereby declare that I am the person who executed the foregoing
Articles of Incorporation, which execution is my act and deed.


                    [illegible]
            (Signature of Incorporator)
